inference_unit.notations: - $fcc$: face-centred cubic - $hcp$: hexagonal close packed - $YH_{x}$: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio $x$ - $P6_{3}/mmc$: space group symmetry identifier for a specific hexagonal crystal structure - $G$: Gibbs free energy - $T$: temperature - $P$: pressure

premise.title: Predicted Stability of fcc-YH10 at High Pressure

premises.premise: Theoretical $ab$ $initio$ calculations predict that the face-centred cubic ($fcc$) phase of yttrium decahydride ($YH_{10}$) reaches thermodynamic stability at an applied pressure of 250 GPa. [23]

Theoretical $ab$ $initio$ calculations suggest that although yttrium decahydride ($YH_{10}$) may be thermodynamically unstable at 0 K, the difference in Gibbs free energies ($\Delta G$) between the face-centred cubic ($fcc$) $YH_{10}$ phase and the hexagonal close packed ($hcp$) $YH_{9}$ phase decreases as temperature increases, such that $fcc-YH_{10}$ is predicted to become more thermodynamically favourable than $hcp-YH_{9}$ at temperatures exceeding 1500 K at a pressure of 375 GPa, or at temperatures exceeding 1100 K at a pressure of 400 GPa. [22]

In high-pressure chemical synthesis experiments using ammonia borane ($NH_{3}BH_{3}$) as a hydrogen source and yttrium trihydride ($YH_{3}$) as a reactant, the successful realization of an excess hydrogen ($H_2$) environment is verified if the initial $YH_{3}$ phase undergoes a complete structural transformation into a hydrogen-rich, single-phase $YH_{9}$ with $P6_{3}/mmc$ symmetry following laser heating.

premise.title: Identification of Crystal Phases via Diffraction

premises.premise: The identification of specific yttrium hydride phases, such as the $P6_{3}/mmc$ $YH_{9}$ phase, is established by demonstrating structural agreement between experimental X-ray powder diffraction patterns and calculated diffraction peak positions derived from theoretical crystal structure models. [22]
